ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for braised beef rice noodle specifically; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers cooked rice noodles as the base component; no composite braised-beef-rice-noodle dish code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Mapped to closest defensible base-food code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for braised beef rice noodle dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Signature Braised Beef Rice Noodle weighs approximately 400 g, comprising rice noodles, braised beef slices, broth, and vegetables, as typically served at a Singaporean Chinese noodle stall.

nutrient_source.title: Signature Braised Beef Rice Noodle 唐小姐红烧牛肉米线 — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010489). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for braised beef (NDB 13365) proportionally adjusted to the dish fat content of 4.21 g/100g; estimated saturated fat ≈ 1.6 g/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles, cooked (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific branded dish. GI of 61 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (mean GI ≈ 61, medium category), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 25.19 × 4.0) / 100 ≈ 61.5 for a 400 g bowl (carbohydrate ≈ 100.8 g per serving). Braising sauce and beef protein content may modestly lower the effective GI; this estimate is therefore conservative.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; full bowl (400 g) delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, representing 40–46% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced broth or sauce, as the braising liquid concentrates sodium; consuming only half the broth can reduce sodium intake by an estimated 30–40% per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g with medium GI (≈61); a standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 101 g carbohydrate, requiring careful portion management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to leverage the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre content, which attenuates postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 1.6 g/100g from braised beef; sodium at 231 mg/100g; red meat consumption is associated with elevated LDL-cholesterol risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency to once weekly, request less broth to reduce sodium load, and consider substituting beef with a leaner protein such as chicken or tofu to reduce saturated fat and LDL-raising potential.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Braised beef is red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of beef; substituting with chicken or tofu-based noodle dishes is recommended for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Signature Braised Beef Rice Noodle (唐小姐红烧牛肉米线) is a Chinese-style spicy noodle dish served in Singapore, featuring tender slices of slow-braised beef served over smooth rice noodles (米线, mǐxiàn) in a rich, aromatic red-braised broth. The braising liquid is built from soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, star anise, cinnamon, dried chilli, and rock sugar, producing a deeply savoury, mildly sweet, and moderately spicy flavour profile. The rice noodles are soft and silky with a slightly chewy bite, absorbing the umami-rich broth. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The notable fibre content (3.7 g/100g) from noodles and vegetables moderates the glycaemic response despite the medium GI of approximately 61. Potassium at 327 mg/100g and iron at 2.55 mg/100g are clinically relevant micronutrients. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium from braising sauce and broth, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to carbohydrate load in a standard serving, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to red meat saturated fat. It is unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of beef. The dish is a popular lunch and dinner option at Singaporean Chinese noodle stalls and is associated with the brand 唐小姐 (Miss Tang).

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010489, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy braised beef proportionally adjusted; this single estimated field does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong primary source for all other fields. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rice noodles, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.6 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for braised beef (NDB 13365), proportionally adjusted to the dish fat content of 4.21 g/100g; HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite braised beef rice noodle dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB source, though it is physiologically unusual for a beef-containing dish — it may reflect the per-100g dilution across the full dish weight including broth and noodles. 4. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this branded dish. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains beef and soy sauce — consumers should verify halal certification with the vendor. 6. The dish is described as spicy (辣); actual spice level may vary by preparation.
